nativeRange.habitat: Slow-moving or standing waters of streams, ditches, ponds, and rice paddies; often grows emersed along margins and tolerates long periods fully submerged

decorPreferences: tolerates any substrate (sand, gravel, or nutrient-rich aquasoil) — but grows faster and fuller in nutrient-rich substrate
best displayed in groups of multiple stems for a bushy stand
needs open water above for vertical growth

avoidWith: plant-eating fish such as goldfish, silver dollars, large herbivorous cichlids

summary: The Dwarf Hygro (Hygrophila polysperma) is widely regarded as one of the hardiest and fastest-growing stem plants in the freshwater hobby, tolerating a very wide range of light, CO2, and water conditions. LEGALLY RESTRICTED in the US — listed as a USDA Federal Noxious Weed and banned or restricted in several states.
careNotes: Substrate is flexible — sand, gravel, or nutrient-rich aquasoil all work; nutrient-rich substrate paired with moderate fertilisation will give noticeably fuller, faster growth, though the plant grows acceptably without it. Reaches 20-30+ cm in typical aquarium conditions and up to ~60 cm (24 in) under good light, so it is best placed as a background or tall-midground plant; frequent pruning is generally advised to control height and encourage lateral branching. Leaf colour and shape vary substantially with light: under low light the leaves are larger, paler green, and more elongated; under brighter light they become smaller, more compact, and often develop reddish/coppery tones at the tips. IMPORTANT LEGAL/REGULATORY WARNING: Hygrophila polysperma is listed on the USDA APHIS Federal Noxious Weed List in the United States — it is illegal to import, sell, or transport across state lines without a federal permit. It is additionally banned or restricted at the state level in Florida (Prohibited Aquatic Plant), Kansas, South Carolina, Texas, and several others; it has naturalised in Florida, Texas, and parts of the southern US. Hobbyists in regulated jurisdictions should choose alternative stem plants such as Hygrophila corymbosa, Hygrophila difformis, or Bacopa species. Even where legal, never dispose of trimmings into waterways or municipal drains — fragment-based spread is the main vector for this plant's invasion.
breedingNotes: Propagation is trivial: pinch or cut a healthy stem 8-15 cm long, remove the lowest pair of leaves, and push the bare end into the substrate — new roots form rapidly. Detached leaves floating at the surface have also been reported to spontaneously form small plantlets. This ease of fragment propagation is precisely why the species is so invasive in the wild, and why trimmings must be bagged and discarded with household waste rather than released into any natural waterway.

primary.name: Tropica
primary.notes: Primary source. Tropica describes Hygrophila polysperma as a stem plant from South-East Asia, easy difficulty, low light demand, low CO2 demand, fast growth, 20-30+ cm height (4-8 cm width), and 'one of the hardiest aquarium plants available'. Manifest URL (Hygrophilapolysperma(037)/4681) returned the generic Tropica landing page; the working canonical Tropica species page is /plantdetails/4483/4483 (located via Tropica's own search results). The 'Rosanervig' / Sunset cultivar is documented on a separate Tropica page (/plantdetails/Hygrophilapolysperma'Rosanervig'(050B)/4484) — folded into this entry per hybrid-consolidation policy.

additional.name: Fish Laboratory
additional.notes: Secondary source. Used to cross-check temperature (64-86°F / 18-30°C), pH (5.0-8.0), max height (up to 24 in / 60 cm), fast growth rate, substrate flexibility (sand/gravel/aquarium soil), low-to-high lighting tolerance, beginner difficulty, and the USDA Federal Noxious Weed / Florida-prohibited status. Buce Plant (the originally designated secondary) returned HTTP 404 for the product page — swapped to Fish Laboratory.

additional.name: Wikipedia
additional.notes: Used for taxonomy (Acanthaceae, Lamiales), full synonymy (Justicia polysperma, Hemidelphis polysperma), expanded native range (Bangladesh, India, China, Malaysia), expanded common-name list (dwarf hygrophila, dwarf hygro, Miramar weed, Indian swampweed, Indian waterweed), and US invasive/regulatory status (Federal Noxious Weed List, Kansas and South Carolina restrictions, naturalised in Florida, Texas, possibly Virginia).

additional.name: Tropica — Hygrophila polysperma 'Rosanervig'
additional.notes: Folded cultivar source per hybrid-consolidation policy. Distinguishes 'Sunset'/'Rosanervig' from the type form: medium CO2 (6-14 mg/L), medium growth rate, pink marbled leaves with creamy-white veins caused by a benign viroid; the viroid does not infect other aquarium plants. Otherwise undemanding and easy.

propagation: leaf plantlets at the water surface

hobbyistAdvisory.reason: Listed on the USDA APHIS Federal Noxious Weed List in the United States; illegal to import, sell, or transport across state lines without a federal permit. Additionally banned/restricted at the state level in Florida, Kansas, South Carolina, Texas, and others. Naturalised invasive in Florida and Texas waterways.
